---
title: "14 · Conditional Probability and Independence"
subtitle: "What changes when you know more — and Bayes' rule"
lesson-id: "t3.conditional-probability"
tier: 3
prerequisites: ["t3.basic-probability"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [13 · Basic Probability](../13-basic-probability/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with sample spaces and $P(E) = \text{favourable}/\text{total}$?
3. Comfortable with inclusion-exclusion and complement rules?
:::

## Touch

Pick the disease prevalence and a test's accuracy. The widget computes
$P(\text{disease} \mid \text{positive test})$ — the conditional
probability that you actually have the disease, given a positive
result. The answer is often shockingly low.

```{ojs}
viewof prev = Inputs.range([0.001, 0.10], {value: 0.01, step: 0.001, label: "Disease prevalence (P(D)):"})
viewof sens = Inputs.range([0.80, 1.0], {value: 0.99, step: 0.01, label: "Test sensitivity (P(+|D)):"})
viewof spec = Inputs.range([0.80, 1.0], {value: 0.95, step: 0.01, label: "Test specificity (P(−|¬D)):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const pD = prev;
  const pNotD = 1 - pD;
  const pPosGivenD = sens;
  const pPosGivenNotD = 1 - spec;
  const pPos = pPosGivenD * pD + pPosGivenNotD * pNotD;
  const pDGivenPos = (pPosGivenD * pD) / pPos;
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="text-align:center; font-family:monospace; font-size:1.2rem;">
      P(D | +) = ${(pPosGivenD * pD).toFixed(4)} / ${pPos.toFixed(4)}
      = <strong style="color:#c2185b;">${(pDGivenPos * 100).toFixed(1)}%</strong>
    </div>
    <div style="text-align:center; font-size:0.95rem; margin-top:0.5rem; color:#666;">
      With a ${(prev*100).toFixed(1)}% prevalence and a ${(sens*100).toFixed(0)}%-sensitive,
      ${(spec*100).toFixed(0)}%-specific test, only ${(pDGivenPos * 100).toFixed(1)}%
      of positive results indicate true disease.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

A positive test on a "99% accurate" screening for a 1% prevalent
disease gives only **17% probability of actually being sick**. Set
the sliders. Watch the surprise. This is **Bayes' theorem** at work.

## Wonder

1. P(disease | positive) — why isn't it 99%?
2. What changes if disease prevalence is 50% instead of 1%?
3. Two events $A, B$ are **independent** if knowing $A$ doesn't change
   the probability of $B$. Examples?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Two cards are drawn (without replacement) from a 52-card deck.
P(second is red **given** first is red)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
After removing one red card, 51 cards remain — 25 red.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(second red | first red) = 25/51 ≈ 0.490.

(Without conditioning, P(red) = 26/52 = 0.5. The condition lowers it
slightly because one red was already taken.)

**Notation:** $P(A | B)$ means "probability of $A$ given $B$" — the
*conditional* probability.
</details>

### Problem 2

Define $P(A | B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$. Verify with the card
problem.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P(A \cap B)$ = P(both red) = $\dfrac{26}{52} \cdot \dfrac{25}{51}$.
$P(B)$ = P(first red) = 26/52.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(A \cap B) = \dfrac{26}{52} \cdot \dfrac{25}{51} = \dfrac{650}{2652}$.
$P(B) = \dfrac{26}{52}$.
$\dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \dfrac{25}{51}$ ✓.

The definition formalises "given B, restrict the sample space to B
and re-normalise."
</details>

### Problem 3

Two events $A, B$ are **independent** if $P(A | B) = P(A)$
(equivalently, $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$).

Are coin tosses independent? Are card draws **without replacement**
independent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Coin tosses:** independent. The first toss tells you nothing about
the second.

**Card draws (no replacement):** **not** independent — Problem 1
showed P(red | red) = 25/51, not P(red) = 26/52. The first draw
*affects* the second.

**Card draws *with* replacement:** independent. After replacing the
card, the deck is restored.
</details>

### Problem 4

Apply the definition: P(A | B) · P(B) = P(A ∩ B). And by symmetry,
P(B | A) · P(A) = P(A ∩ B). Equate: $P(A|B) P(B) = P(B|A) P(A)$, giving:
$$P(A | B) = \dfrac{P(B | A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}.$$
This is **Bayes' theorem.**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Direct algebra from the definition. The theorem is named after
Thomas Bayes (1701–1761).

**Importance:** lets us "flip" a conditional probability. Often P(B
| A) is easy to know (sensitivity of a test, frequency of a symptom
given a disease) but P(A | B) is what we *want* to know (probability
of disease given a positive test, probability of a hypothesis given
data).
</details>

### Problem 5

A disease has prevalence 1% (P(D) = 0.01). A test has sensitivity 99%
(P(+|D) = 0.99) and specificity 95% (P(−|¬D) = 0.95, so P(+|¬D) =
0.05). You test positive. What is P(D | +)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply Bayes: $P(D|+) = \dfrac{P(+|D) P(D)}{P(+)}$.
$P(+) = P(+|D) P(D) + P(+|\neg D) P(\neg D) = 0.99 \cdot 0.01 + 0.05
\cdot 0.99 \approx 0.059$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(D|+) = \dfrac{0.99 \cdot 0.01}{0.0594} \approx 0.167$ — only
**17%**.

The reason: with 1% prevalence and a 5% false-positive rate, *most*
positives come from healthy people (because there are 99× more of
them). Even with a "99% accurate" test, the rare disease and the
common false-positive together make positive results unreliable.

This is the **base rate fallacy** — and it explains why screening
tests for rare conditions need follow-up confirmation.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Thomas Bayes** (1701–1761), an English Presbyterian minister, never
published the theorem now bearing his name. After his death, his
friend Richard Price found the manuscript among his papers and
submitted it to the Royal Society as *"An Essay towards solving a
Problem in the Doctrine of Chances"* (**1763**). The essay was largely
ignored for two centuries — then resurrected in the 20th century to
become the backbone of medical statistics, machine learning, and the
spam filter that protects your inbox today.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Conditioning shrinks the sample space.** "Given $B$" simply throws
away every outcome where $B$ didn't happen, then re-normalises the
rest. Bayes' theorem is the algebraic price of switching which event
you stand inside.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Conditional probability:** $P(A | B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$,
provided $P(B) > 0$.

**Multiplication rule:** $P(A \cap B) = P(A | B) \cdot P(B) = P(B | A) \cdot P(A)$.

**Independence:** $A$ and $B$ are independent iff
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B), \quad \text{equivalently} \quad P(A | B) = P(A).$$

**Bayes' theorem:**
$$P(A | B) = \dfrac{P(B | A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}.$$

**Law of total probability:** if $\{B_1, \ldots, B_k\}$ partitions
the sample space,
$$P(A) = \sum_{i} P(A | B_i) P(B_i).$$

:::

> *Conditional probability restricts to the conditioning event;*
> *Independence: knowing one tells you nothing about the other;*
> *Bayes' theorem flips $P(A|B)$ into $P(B|A)$ — the inference rule.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A factory has two machines. M1 produces 60% of parts, with 2%
> defective. M2 produces 40%, with 5% defective. A defective part is
> found. What is the probability it came from M1?

A competent solver applies Bayes' theorem:

1. **Setup:** $P(M_1) = 0.6, P(M_2) = 0.4, P(D|M_1) = 0.02, P(D|M_2) =
   0.05$.
2. **Total:** $P(D) = 0.02 \cdot 0.6 + 0.05 \cdot 0.4 = 0.012 + 0.02 =
   0.032$.
3. **Bayes:** $P(M_1 | D) = \dfrac{0.02 \cdot 0.6}{0.032} = \dfrac{0.012}{0.032}
   = 0.375$.

**Answer: 37.5%.** Despite M1 producing more parts overall, M2
contributes more to defective parts (proportionally). Posterior
probabilities flip your intuition.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** P(rolling 6 | rolling even) on a die?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
P(6 ∩ even)/P(even) = (1/6)/(3/6) = 1/3.
</details>

**W2.** Two coin tosses. Independent? P(2nd H | 1st H)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Independent. P(2nd H | 1st H) = P(2nd H) = 1/2.
</details>

**W3.** Bayes: $P(A|B) = ?$ given $P(A) = 0.3, P(B|A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(A|B) = (0.4 \cdot 0.3)/0.5 = 0.24$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Draw two without
replacement. P(both red)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P(R_1 \cap R_2) = P(R_1) \cdot P(R_2 | R_1) = (4/10)(3/9)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$= 12/90 = 2/15 \approx 0.133$.
</details>

**P2.** Email spam filter: P(spam) = 0.4. P(contains "free" | spam)
= 0.7. P(contains "free" | not spam) = 0.05. An email contains "free."
P(spam | "free")?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Bayes. P("free") = 0.7·0.4 + 0.05·0.6 = 0.31.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(\text{spam} | \text{"free"}) = \dfrac{0.7 \cdot 0.4}{0.31} \approx 0.903$.

About **90%**. Modern spam filters (Naive Bayes, originally) use
Bayes' theorem on many keywords simultaneously.
</details>

**P3.** A and B are independent with P(A) = 0.3, P(B) = 0.5. P(A or B)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Inclusion-exclusion: $P(A) + P(B) - P(A) P(B)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.3 + 0.5 - 0.15 = 0.65$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Monty Hall problem.** Three doors. Behind one: a car. Behind
the other two: goats. You pick door A. The host (who knows where the
car is) opens door B revealing a goat and offers you the chance to
switch to door C. Should you switch?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
P(car behind A) = 1/3. P(car behind B or C) = 2/3. After host opens
B (always a goat), where is that 2/3 concentrated?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Switch.** P(car behind A) is still 1/3 (the host's reveal didn't
change it — he was forced to open a goat door). The remaining 2/3
probability is now entirely on door C.

So P(win | stay) = 1/3, P(win | switch) = 2/3. Switching doubles your
chances.

This problem gained fame in the 1990s when Marilyn vos Savant
correctly explained it in *Parade* magazine and was deluged with
angry letters from PhDs (including Paul Erdős!) insisting she was
wrong. She wasn't. Conditional probability defies intuition.
</details>

**S2.** **Naive Bayes classifier.** With features $x_1, \ldots, x_n$
and class $C$, assume features are *conditionally independent* given
$C$:
$$P(C | x_1, \ldots, x_n) \propto P(C) \prod_i P(x_i | C).$$
Why is this useful — and why "naive"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Useful:** turns a high-dimensional joint probability into a product
of one-dimensional conditionals — vastly simpler to estimate and
compute.

**Naive:** real features are usually *not* conditionally independent
(in spam filtering, "free" and "money" co-occur more than independence
predicts). Yet Naive Bayes works *surprisingly well* in practice — for
many tasks, the assumption errs only on probability magnitudes, not
on which class wins.

Naive Bayes is one of the oldest and still useful classifiers,
especially as a baseline. It exemplifies a common ML strategy: make a
simplifying assumption, derive a tractable model, and let empirical
performance vindicate it.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$P(A|B) = P(B|A)$."** **Almost never.** Bayes' theorem relates
   them: $P(A|B) = P(B|A) \cdot P(A)/P(B)$. Confusing the two is the
   *prosecutor's fallacy* (P(evidence|innocent) ≠ P(innocent|evidence)).
2. **"Independent events have nothing to do with each other."** They
   *can* both occur — they're just probabilistically uncorrelated.
   Don't confuse with mutually exclusive (which means they *cannot*
   both occur).
3. **"P(B|A) is always smaller than P(B)."** Not necessarily — it can
   be larger or smaller, depending on whether $A$ makes $B$ more or
   less likely.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **[Conditional probability](/glossary.qmd#conditional-probability)
   restricts the [sample space](/glossary.qmd#sample-space) to "given
   $B$" and re-normalises** — that's the entire definition;
   everything else is bookkeeping.
2. **[Bayes' theorem](/glossary.qmd#bayes-theorem) is how you flip a
   conditional.** What you can measure ($P(\text{symptom} \mid
   \text{disease})$) is rarely what you want ($P(\text{disease} \mid
   \text{symptom})$); Bayes is the bridge.
3. **Independence is a fragile gift.** If $A$ and $B$ are independent,
   joint [probability](/glossary.qmd#probability) factors —
   $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$. Most real-world events are *not*
   independent, which is why "naive" assumptions get you in trouble.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state Bayes' theorem and apply it to a simple disease-
   testing scenario?
2. Without scrolling back, are coin tosses independent? Card draws
   without replacement?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answers: yes; no.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[15 · Discrete Distributions](../15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (Monty Hall) — most-debated problem in
  popular probability.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P4 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[13 · Basic Probability](../13-basic-probability/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[15 · Discrete Distributions](../15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)** —
  full probability functions on values.
- **Tier 5 — Statistics & Probability Theory** — Bayes' theorem in
  full generality (continuous variables).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Naive Bayes (S2) is a classic classifier.
**Bayesian inference** is an entire approach to ML — every model is a
*posterior probability* updated from data. Bayesian deep learning is
an active research area.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Side-channel attacks use Bayes' theorem on partial
observations to recover keys. Probabilistic security proofs are
foundational.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Medicine & policy.** Diagnostic test interpretation (S5) requires
Bayes thinking. Public-health policy on screening is shaped by base-
rate considerations.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Law & forensics.** The prosecutor's fallacy (confusion 1) has
caused multiple high-profile miscarriages of justice. Sound legal
reasoning needs Bayes thinking.
:::
